title: NATO's Doctrinal Crisis: Combined-Arms Maneuver Under Fire

source.original: General Valerii Zaluzhnyi on the Impossibility of Operational-Level Tasks Due to Technological Progress — Giorgi Revishvili Thread

body_html:
NATO's operational doctrine still rests heavily on the concept of combined-arms maneuver — the synchronized employment of armor, infantry, artillery, aviation, and logistics to achieve breakthrough and exploitation at the operational level. Zaluzhnyi's assertion that this is no longer possible strikes directly at the alliance's doctrinal core.

The challenge is not merely theoretical. NATO's force structure, training pipelines, and procurement programs are all built around the assumption that large formations will maneuver decisively in a contested environment. Brigade combat teams, armored divisions, and corps-level headquarters exist precisely to plan and execute the kind of operational-level tasks that Zaluzhnyi says technology has rendered impossible.

If the defense-dominant, transparency-saturated battlefield observed in Ukraine is the new norm rather than an anomaly, NATO faces a doctrinal crisis that cannot be solved by incremental upgrades. Buying more tanks or faster helicopters does not address the fundamental problem that massing forces invites destruction. The alliance would need to shift toward distributed operations, smaller and more autonomous units, and a concept of victory built around attrition and resilience rather than decisive maneuver.

This debate is already underway in defense circles, but institutional inertia is powerful. Armies are slow to abandon the doctrines around which their identities and career structures are organized. The risk is that NATO enters its next major conflict with a force designed for a style of warfare that the battlefield no longer permits.

Zaluzhnyi's voice adds urgency to this conversation precisely because he is not a critic from outside the system. He tried to execute combined-arms operations with Western-trained brigades and found the environment unforgiving. That operational verdict demands a serious doctrinal response.
